longTailKeywords: what kind of arthritis do I have
how to know which type of arthritis
_meta.authoringNotes: Umbrella SEO page covering the seven major arthritis families. Numbers cross-referenced against CDC NHIS 2019-2021, ACR 2020-2023 guidelines, EULAR 2023 recommendations, GBD 2019 musculoskeletal data, and the Lancet 2021 osteoarthritis review (Hunter & Bierma-Zeinstra). Drug doses, screening cutoffs and crystallography facts verified against UpToDate/Lexicomp current as of 2026-05.

h1Title: Arthritis: Types, Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Arthritis is an umbrella term for more than 100 conditions that share a single feature: inflammation, damage, or pain inside a joint. The five families that cause most cases are osteoarthritis (the wear-and-tear form), rheumatoid arthritis (a systemic autoimmune disease), gout (crystal-driven), the spondyloarthropathies (psoriatic, ankylosing, reactive), and juvenile idiopathic arthritis in children. The CDC estimates that 58.5 million US adults (24%) have doctor-diagnosed arthritis, with prevalence projected to reach 78 million by 2040. Presentation varies sharply by type, but the unifying symptoms are pain, stiffness, swelling, and reduced range of motion. Identifying which type of arthritis a patient has is the central diagnostic task because treatments diverge completely, and several types are fully controllable with modern therapy.

keyStats.prevalence: 58.5 million US adults (23.7%) doctor-diagnosed; projected 78.4 million by 2040 (CDC NHIS 2019-2021)
keyStats.demographics: Women affected more often than men (26% vs 19% US adults); rates rise sharply after age 45
keyStats.avgAge: Onset varies by type: OA typically after 50, RA between 30-60, JIA before age 16, gout in men 30-50 and women 60+
keyStats.globalCases: Over 595 million people worldwide live with osteoarthritis alone (GBD 2019); rheumatoid arthritis affects about 18 million globally

definition: Arthritis (ICD-10: M19.90 for unspecified arthritis, with type-specific codes from M00 to M19) is any disease process that produces inflammation or structural damage in one or more joints. Mechanistically, the major categories are distinct: osteoarthritis is a mechanical and biochemical breakdown of articular cartilage and underlying bone; rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune attack on the synovial lining driven by anti-citrullinated peptide antibodies; gout and pseudogout are crystal-deposition diseases; the spondyloarthropathies are HLA-B27 associated inflammatory diseases of entheses and axial joints; reactive arthritis follows certain infections; septic arthritis is direct bacterial invasion of the joint space; and juvenile idiopathic arthritis is the umbrella for chronic childhood arthritides. Each type has its own diagnostic criteria, characteristic joint distribution, lab signature, imaging pattern, and treatment pathway. All forms share final-pathway symptoms — pain, stiffness, swelling, warmth, and progressive loss of joint function — which is why patients arrive at the same waiting room before the workup begins. Rheumatology is the parent specialty; orthopedics, primary care, sports medicine and pediatric rheumatology manage specific subsets.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, affecting about 32.5 million US adults. Driven by cartilage breakdown, subchondral bone changes and low-grade synovial inflammation. Typically appears after age 50, worsens with use, and most often affects knees, hips, hands and spine. Pain peaks with activity and improves with rest.

A systemic autoimmune disease affecting roughly 1.3 million US adults and about 18 million people worldwide. Causes symmetric inflammation of small joints in the hands and feet, with morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are typically positive. Without treatment, leads to erosive joint damage; modern DMARDs can induce remission in over half of patients.

Caused by deposition of crystals inside joints. Gout (monosodium urate) affects about 4% of US adults; pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate) is more common after age 65. Both present as sudden, intense, monoarticular flares — gout classically in the big toe, pseudogout in the knee or wrist.

A spondyloarthropathy affecting roughly 30% of people with psoriasis, totaling about 1 million US adults. Distinctive features include dactylitis (sausage digit), enthesitis, nail pitting and asymmetric joint involvement. May involve peripheral joints, the spine or both.

Primarily inflames the sacroiliac joints and spine, producing inflammatory back pain that improves with activity. Onset usually before age 45, more common in men, strongly linked to the HLA-B27 gene. Untreated, can lead to spinal fusion over decades.

The umbrella term for chronic arthritis starting before age 16. Affects about 1 in 1,000 US children. Subtypes include oligoarticular, polyarticular, systemic (Still disease), enthesitis-related and psoriatic. Uveitis screening is mandatory in several subtypes. Many children reach drug-free remission with prompt DMARD therapy.

Septic arthritis is direct bacterial infection of a joint, typically by Staphylococcus aureus; it is an emergency requiring urgent drainage and intravenous antibiotics. Reactive arthritis develops 1-4 weeks after a gastrointestinal or urogenital infection (Salmonella, Shigella, Chlamydia, Yersinia) and usually resolves over 3-12 months.

primarySymptoms: Joint pain that may be activity-related (typical of osteoarthritis), worse at rest and overnight (typical of inflammatory types), or sudden and severe (typical of crystal or septic arthritis).
Morning stiffness — under 30 minutes in osteoarthritis; over an hour in rheumatoid, psoriatic and ankylosing forms; pattern alone narrows the differential by half.
Visible joint swelling and warmth, sometimes with bright redness in crystal or septic arthritis; redness is unusual in plain osteoarthritis.
Reduced range of motion and grip strength that interferes with buttons, jar lids, stairs or putting on socks; loss of function is often the first thing patients notice.
Symmetric small-joint involvement in the hands and feet suggests rheumatoid arthritis; asymmetric involvement with nail pitting points toward psoriatic arthritis.
Inflammatory low back and buttock pain in adults under 45, improving with exercise and worse at night, suggests axial spondyloarthritis.
Sausage-shaped swelling of an entire finger or toe (dactylitis) is highly specific for psoriatic or reactive arthritis.
A single hot, exquisitely tender, red joint that peaks in 6-24 hours suggests gout, pseudogout or septic arthritis until proven otherwise.
Crackling, grinding or clicking with movement (crepitus) is common in osteoarthritis of the knee, hip and base of the thumb.
Systemic features — low-grade fever, fatigue, weight loss, rash, eye redness or dry mouth — point to inflammatory or autoimmune types rather than osteoarthritis.
earlyWarningSigns: Stiffness in the hands or feet on waking that takes more than 30 minutes to wear off, repeatedly, over six weeks
Joint pain that disturbs sleep or wakes the patient in the second half of the night
Inflammatory back pain before age 45 — better with exercise, worse with rest, improving with NSAIDs
A new, persistent swelling of a single joint without a clear injury
Unexplained fatigue, low-grade fever or weight loss accompanying any joint symptoms
emergencySigns: A hot, swollen, intensely painful single joint with fever above 38.5 C — septic arthritis must be excluded urgently by joint aspiration; delay over 24-48 hours risks permanent cartilage destruction
New numbness, weakness, bladder or bowel changes with back pain — possible cord compression or cauda equina syndrome
Sudden severe eye pain, redness, light sensitivity or blurred vision in someone with spondyloarthritis — acute anterior uveitis requires same-day ophthalmology
Chest pain or shortness of breath with active inflammatory arthritis — possible pericarditis, pleuritis or vasculitis flare
Rapidly worsening rash, mouth sores or skin peeling within 8 weeks of starting allopurinol, sulfasalazine or methotrexate — possible Stevens-Johnson syndrome or DRESS

causes.description: Underlies osteoarthritis. Repetitive load, prior joint injury, obesity and age-related changes overwhelm chondrocyte repair capacity, leading to cartilage thinning, subchondral bone sclerosis and osteophyte formation.

Drives rheumatoid arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis and lupus arthritis. B cells produce anti-citrullinated peptide antibodies and rheumatoid factor; T cells and cytokines (TNF, IL-6, IL-1) attack the synovial membrane, causing pannus formation and bone erosion.

Monosodium urate crystals cause gout; calcium pyrophosphate crystals cause pseudogout. The NLRP3 inflammasome recognizes crystals as danger signals, triggers IL-1 beta release and produces explosive joint inflammation.

Underlies ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis and enteropathic arthritis. HLA-B27 and IL-17/23 pathway dysregulation drive inflammation at entheses (where tendons insert into bone) and the sacroiliac joints.

Bacteria — most often Staphylococcus aureus, also Streptococcus and Neisseria gonorrhoeae — reach the joint through bloodstream seeding, direct trauma or extension from adjacent osteomyelitis. The infection destroys cartilage rapidly without prompt drainage and antibiotics.
Certain gastrointestinal (Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia, Campylobacter) and urogenital (Chlamydia) infections trigger a sterile arthritis 1-4 weeks later, particularly in HLA-B27 carriers.

Heritability is substantial across types: ~50% for rheumatoid arthritis, ~70% for ankylosing spondylitis, ~50% for gout. HLA-DRB1 shared epitope predicts more aggressive RA; HLA-B27 dominates the spondyloarthropathies.

riskFactors.description: Osteoarthritis prevalence rises from 8% at age 45 to over 40% after age 75 (CDC). Other types have characteristic onset windows but most arthritis caseload comes from the older end of the population.
Women carry a 2-3x higher risk of rheumatoid arthritis, lupus and hand osteoarthritis. Hormonal and immunological differences are leading explanations.
A first-degree relative with rheumatoid arthritis raises risk roughly 3-5x; HLA-B27 carriers have a 5-6% lifetime risk of ankylosing spondylitis versus 0.5% in non-carriers.

Each 5 kg/m² increase in BMI raises knee osteoarthritis risk roughly 35%, gout incidence by about 20%, and worsens response to psoriatic and rheumatoid therapies. Weight loss of 5-10% measurably lowers symptoms across types.
ACL tears, meniscal tears, fractures involving the joint surface and recurrent dislocations raise post-traumatic osteoarthritis risk roughly 4-6x in the affected joint over the next 10-20 years.

Doubles the risk of rheumatoid arthritis in anti-CCP-positive individuals, worsens psoriatic disease activity, and predicts poorer response to TNF inhibitors. Quitting reduces RA risk over 10-20 years.
Repetitive squatting, kneeling, heavy lifting and high-impact sports raise lifetime osteoarthritis risk, particularly at the knee and hip.
Independently associated with osteoarthritis, gout and increased cardiovascular complications across inflammatory arthritis types.
Approximately 30% of people with psoriasis develop psoriatic arthritis; nail involvement and scalp psoriasis predict higher risk.

riskFactors.factor: Recent gastrointestinal or sexually transmitted infection
riskFactors.description: Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia, Campylobacter and Chlamydia infections can trigger reactive arthritis within 1-4 weeks, especially in HLA-B27 carriers.

affectedDemographics: US: 58.5 million adults with doctor-diagnosed arthritis; 25.7 million report activity limitations because of it (CDC NHIS 2019-2021)
Women have higher rates of most arthritis types except gout, ankylosing spondylitis and reactive arthritis, which are male-predominant
Black and Hispanic adults in the US report higher arthritis-attributable activity limitation than white adults, even at similar prevalence — reflecting access and severity disparities
Indigenous populations (Native American, Maori, Pacific Islander) carry the highest rheumatoid arthritis and gout prevalence globally, partly due to genetic background
Childhood-onset arthritis (JIA) affects roughly 1 in 1,000 children worldwide; girls outnumber boys 2-3:1 in oligoarticular and polyarticular subtypes
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing arthritis is a two-step process: confirm that joint inflammation or structural damage is present, then identify which type. The workup begins with a focused history — pattern of joint involvement, age at onset, stiffness duration, presence of swelling or redness, systemic symptoms, family history, psoriasis, eye disease and recent infection. A targeted physical examination follows: counting tender and swollen joints, examining the entheses, checking spinal range of motion (Schober test), looking for nail pitting and skin disease. First-line blood tests typically include rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP antibodies, ESR, CRP, complete blood count, serum urate and a basic metabolic panel. HLA-B27 is added when spondyloarthropathy is suspected, ANA when lupus is suspected. Plain X-rays document chronic damage but are normal in early inflammatory disease; ultrasound and MRI detect synovitis, erosions and enthesitis months to years earlier than radiography. Joint aspiration is decisive for a hot, swollen monoarticular joint — synovial fluid analysis with polarized microscopy, Gram stain, culture and cell count distinguishes septic arthritis from gout, pseudogout and inflammatory effusions. The ACR/EULAR classification criteria for rheumatoid arthritis, the ASAS criteria for axial spondyloarthritis, the CASPAR criteria for psoriatic arthritis and the 2015 ACR/EULAR gout criteria standardize this work. Treat-to-target is the modern principle across the inflammatory types: confirm the diagnosis early, start disease-modifying therapy within the first three months when possible, and adjust until clinical remission or low disease activity is reached.

diagnosticTests.test: Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes rheumatoid arthritis from other inflammatory arthritides. Anti-CCP has roughly 67% sensitivity and 95% specificity for RA and predicts more aggressive disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw, often combined with other tests. Results typically return within 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify systemic inflammation. Useful to support an inflammatory diagnosis, track disease activity and monitor treatment response. Normal values do not exclude arthritis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw. Results return same day in most labs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hyperuricemia and supports a gout diagnosis. Levels are unreliable during an acute flare — repeat 2-4 weeks after symptoms resolve.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test. Target for treated gout is below 6.0 mg/dL.

diagnosticTests.test: Joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for any hot, swollen joint of uncertain cause. Cell count, Gram stain and culture identify infection; polarized microscopy identifies urate or calcium pyrophosphate crystals.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthetic is injected; a thin needle removes 0.5-2 mL of fluid. The procedure takes 5-10 minutes. Mild soreness for a day is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents joint space narrowing, erosions, osteophytes, sacroiliitis and other chronic changes. Used to grade osteoarthritis severity and to look for erosive changes in RA, PsA and AS.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two-view radiographs of one or more joints. Takes a few minutes; results read same or next day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects synovitis, tenosynovitis, enthesitis, effusions, erosions and crystal deposits earlier than X-ray. Increasingly used as first-line imaging in rheumatology clinics.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician applies gel and scans the joints with a probe for 15-30 minutes. No radiation; painless.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of joints or sacroiliac joints
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bone-marrow oedema, sacroiliitis, synovitis and early erosions invisible on X-ray. Recommended for suspected axial spondyloarthritis and early rheumatoid arthritis with normal X-rays.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lying still in an MRI scanner for 30-60 minutes. Usually no contrast needed for sacroiliitis screening.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Adjunctive when spondyloarthropathy is suspected. Roughly 90% of ankylosing spondylitis patients are HLA-B27 positive; the test alone is not diagnostic because 6-8% of the general population is also positive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results in 3-7 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on the type of arthritis confirmed at diagnosis, but the framework is consistent: control pain, suppress inflammation, prevent structural damage, preserve function, and treat-to-target. Osteoarthritis management is led by exercise, weight loss, physical therapy, topical NSAIDs and oral analgesics, with intra-articular corticosteroid or hyaluronate injections in selected joints and total joint replacement when conservative care fails — current guidelines (ACR 2019, OARSI 2019, NICE NG226 2022) emphasize that exercise and weight loss outperform most drugs in head-to-head trials. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed by starting a conventional synthetic DMARD — methotrexate is first-line for over 80% of patients — within 12 weeks of diagnosis, escalating to biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, B-cell depletion, JAK inhibitors) if treat-to-target is not reached at 3-6 months (ACR 2021, EULAR 2022). Psoriatic arthritis and ankylosing spondylitis follow similar treat-to-target principles with NSAIDs, conventional DMARDs in peripheral PsA, and biologics targeting TNF, IL-17 or IL-23 in moderate-to-severe disease (GRAPPA 2021, ASAS-EULAR 2022). Gout splits into acute-flare management (NSAIDs, colchicine, steroids) and long-term urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol or febuxostat to a serum urate target below 6.0 mg/dL (ACR 2020, NICE NG219 2022). Septic arthritis requires urgent surgical drainage plus 2-6 weeks of culture-directed intravenous and oral antibiotics. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis is treated similarly to RA but with pediatric dosing and uveitis screening; many children reach drug-free remission. Across all types, structured exercise, smoking cessation, vaccination, cardiovascular risk reduction and patient education are non-negotiable adjuncts.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical and oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-800 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily, diclofenac gel)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line symptomatic therapy for most arthritis types. Topical NSAIDs are preferred for hand and knee osteoarthritis to limit systemic exposure. Avoid in CKD stage 3+, peptic ulcer disease, anticoagulation and heart failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meaningful pain reduction in 60-75% of users; numbers needed to treat for a 50% pain reduction in OA are roughly 6-9.

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 20-40 mg, methylprednisolone 40-80 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for one or two symptomatic joints in osteoarthritis, gout, pseudogout and inflammatory arthritis when systemic therapy is contraindicated. Septic arthritis must be excluded before injection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in 65-80% of patients lasting 2-12 weeks; repeated injections in the same joint more often than every 3 months are discouraged in OA.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate (start 10-15 mg orally or subcutaneously weekly; titrate to 25 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Anchor conventional DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Folic acid 5 mg weekly reduces nausea and mouth ulcers. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR50 response in roughly 40-50% of RA patients at 6 months as monotherapy; higher in combination regimens.

medicalTreatments.name: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks, etanercept 50 mg weekly, infliximab IV)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line biologics for RA, PsA and ankylosing spondylitis when conventional DMARDs fail or are inadequate. Pre-treatment screening for tuberculosis and hepatitis B is mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR50 response in 40-60% of RA patients; BASDAI50 response in 50-70% of axial spondyloarthritis patients.

medicalTreatments.name: IL-17 and IL-23 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, guselkumab, risankizumab)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred biologic class in psoriatic arthritis and ankylosing spondylitis, particularly when skin and joint disease coexist or when TNF inhibitors fail.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR50 in 40-55% of PsA patients; high skin clearance rates (PASI 90 in 60-80%).

medicalTreatments.name: Urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol 100-600 mg daily, febuxostat 40-80 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Long-term gout management — dissolves crystal deposits at a sustained urate below 6.0 mg/dL. Screen HLA-B*5801 in Han Chinese, Thai and Korean patients before allopurinol.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reaches target urate in 75-80% of patients with adequate titration; reduces flare frequency by over 80% by year 2.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for septic arthritis (empirical IV ceftriaxone plus vancomycin pending culture)
medicalTreatments.description: Started immediately after joint aspiration, narrowed to culture-directed therapy. Surgical drainage (arthroscopic or open) is performed in parallel. Duration is typically 2-6 weeks depending on organism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Joint salvage in 70-85% with prompt drainage and antibiotics within 24-48 hours; outcomes worsen sharply with delay.

medicalTreatments.name: Physical therapy and structured exercise programs
medicalTreatments.description: Evidence-based for every type of arthritis. Combines aerobic conditioning, strength training, range-of-motion and joint protection. Land-based and aquatic programs are both supported by meta-analyses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction effect sizes (Cohen d) of 0.4-0.6 for knee and hip OA; functional gains comparable to or exceeding pharmacologic therapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Total knee or hip replacement (arthroplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive surgical treatment for end-stage osteoarthritis or inflammatory arthritis when pain and function fail conservative care. Over 1 million knee and hip replacements are performed annually in the US.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction roughly 85-90% at 1 year; implant survival 90-95% at 15 years for modern prostheses.

surgicalOptions.name: Synovectomy (open or arthroscopic)
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of inflamed synovium in refractory RA or hemophilic arthropathy. Less commonly performed since biologic DMARDs became available but still useful for isolated joint disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 60-80% of selected patients for 3-5 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Joint fusion (arthrodesis)
surgicalOptions.description: Considered for advanced wrist, ankle and small-joint arthritis when replacement is not feasible. Eliminates pain by eliminating motion at the joint.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in over 85% of selected patients; permanent loss of joint motion is the trade-off.

surgicalOptions.name: Tophus excision and tendon release for chronic gout
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical debulking of large or ulcerated tophi, or release of nerve compression caused by tophus deposits (carpal or tarsal tunnel).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in 80-90% of selected patients; recurrence is common without strict urate control.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean diet pattern
alternativeTreatments.description: Randomized trials and prospective cohorts show modest reductions in RA disease activity (DAS28) and OA pain scores with sustained adherence to a Mediterranean-style diet rich in olive oil, fish, vegetables and whole grains.

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation (2-3 g daily EPA+DHA)
alternativeTreatments.description: Meta-analyses show small but consistent reductions in tender joint count and morning stiffness in rheumatoid arthritis. Adjunct only; does not replace DMARD therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tai chi and yoga
alternativeTreatments.description: Randomized trials support comparable benefit to conventional physical therapy for knee OA pain and function (Wang 2016). Useful adjuncts that also improve balance and mood.

whySeeSpecialist: A rheumatologist should be consulted whenever inflammatory arthritis is suspected — pattern of involvement is symmetric or includes small joints, morning stiffness lasts over an hour, systemic symptoms are present, or there is family history of autoimmune disease. Early specialist referral changes outcomes: starting a DMARD within the first 12 weeks of rheumatoid arthritis cuts long-term joint damage roughly in half. Refer urgently for a hot single joint with fever (rule out septic arthritis), suspected giant cell arteritis, new uveitis, or rapidly progressive deformity. Most uncomplicated osteoarthritis is managed in primary care with referral to orthopedics for surgical evaluation when conservative care fails.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in rheumatology and experience with treat-to-target protocols, biologic and targeted synthetic DMARDs, musculoskeletal ultrasound, joint aspiration and injection. Ask about access to coordinated care with physical therapy, ophthalmology (for spondyloarthropathy-related uveitis), dermatology (for psoriatic disease) and orthopedics. Continuity matters more than prestige — arthritis is a multi-year relationship, and a clinician who knows the patient's joints and history makes faster and better treatment decisions.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a body mass index below 25 — for every 5 kg/m² above this, knee OA risk rises about 35% and gout incidence climbs roughly 20%
Stay physically active with at least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity weekly plus twice-weekly strength training; inactivity is the strongest reversible OA risk factor
Stop smoking — doubles RA risk in anti-CCP-positive individuals and worsens response to most arthritis medications
Protect joints during sport with proper technique, conditioning and protective gear; prior joint injury is a leading cause of post-traumatic arthritis
Manage cardiometabolic risk factors aggressively — hypertension, type 2 diabetes and dyslipidemia worsen multiple arthritis types and shorten life expectancy in RA and gout
Address oral health — periodontitis is associated with elevated rheumatoid arthritis risk and severity, and treatment of gum disease reduces inflammatory markers
lifestyleModifications: Lose 5-10% of body weight if overweight — produces clinically meaningful reductions in knee OA pain and inflammatory disease activity
Adopt a Mediterranean or DASH-style diet rich in vegetables, olive oil, fish, whole grains and nuts; limit ultra-processed food and added sugar
Exercise regularly with a mix of aerobic, strength and flexibility work; aquatic exercise is excellent during flares of weight-bearing joint disease
Use joint-protection strategies — supportive footwear, ergonomic tools, regular movement breaks, and assistive devices when needed
Track symptoms in a simple log or app — joint count, morning stiffness duration, pain score; share with the clinical team to inform treat-to-target adjustments
Keep vaccinations current, especially influenza, pneumococcus, shingles and COVID-19, particularly before starting biologic or JAK inhibitor therapy
Address sleep — poor sleep increases pain perception and inflammatory markers; treat sleep apnea, anxiety and depression that often coexist
Stop smoking and limit alcohol — both worsen most arthritis types and reduce medication safety

recommended: Olive oil as the primary cooking fat
Oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) twice weekly for omega-3 intake
Leafy greens, cruciferous vegetables and colorful produce for polyphenol intake
Whole grains, legumes, nuts and seeds for fiber and magnesium
Low-fat dairy — modestly uricosuric and protective against gout
Cherries and berries in moderation — observational reduction in gout flare frequency
avoid: Ultra-processed foods, sugar-sweetened beverages and high-fructose corn syrup — promote weight gain, inflammation and hyperuricemia
Excessive red meat and processed meat — limit to 1-2 servings weekly
Beer and spirits at high quantities — both raise gout risk and interact with arthritis medications
Crash diets and prolonged fasting — can trigger gout flares and worsen RA symptoms
Excess sodium — worsens hypertension, a frequent comorbidity
Trans fats and excess saturated fats — drive cardiovascular risk that is already elevated in inflammatory arthritis

exerciseGuidelines: Regular low-impact aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming, elliptical), twice-weekly strength training, and range-of-motion work are recommended across all arthritis types. During an acute flare, switch to gentle range-of-motion and aquatic exercise; resume full programming once swelling settles. Patients with ankylosing spondylitis benefit specifically from daily spinal extension and breathing exercises. A physiotherapist or exercise physiologist with rheumatology experience can build a sustainable program around individual joint involvement.
dailyManagement: Take disease-modifying or urate-lowering medication at the same time daily — adherence is the strongest predictor of remission and flare-free years
Apply heat for stiffness in the morning and ice during a flare; both have small but reliable symptomatic effects
Schedule short movement breaks every 30-60 minutes during sedentary work — prolonged immobility worsens stiffness
Keep a short course of NSAID, colchicine or prednisolone (as prescribed) at home for early flare treatment
Monitor weight monthly and recheck blood pressure, lipids and HbA1c yearly — cardiovascular risk is elevated in inflammatory arthritis
Stay hydrated, especially during hot weather, exercise and illness
prognosis: Outlook depends almost entirely on type and treatment. Osteoarthritis progresses slowly over decades; pain and function can be maintained or improved for most patients through weight management, exercise and timely joint replacement when needed. Rheumatoid arthritis treated with early DMARD therapy and treat-to-target reaches remission or low disease activity in roughly 50-70% of patients, and erosive damage is largely preventable when treatment starts within the first 12 weeks. Psoriatic arthritis and ankylosing spondylitis respond similarly to modern biologic therapy, with substantial reductions in radiographic progression. Gout is fully controllable at a serum urate below 6.0 mg/dL — flares typically reduce by 80% within 2 years. Septic arthritis is the urgent exception: untreated, joint destruction occurs within days and mortality reaches 7-15%; with prompt drainage and antibiotics, joint salvage is 70-85%. Long-term, the inflammatory arthritides carry an increased cardiovascular mortality (RA roughly 1.5x age-matched controls), which is reduced but not eliminated by inflammation control. Across types, patients who reach treat-to-target maintain function and quality of life close to age-matched peers.
recoveryTimeline: Timelines differ by type. Septic arthritis: 2-6 weeks of antibiotics with most clinical response by week 2-4; full functional recovery over 3-6 months. Gout flare: pain peaks at 12-24 hours, with 50% reduction in 1-2 days of treatment and full resolution by day 5-14. Rheumatoid arthritis: meaningful improvement on methotrexate by week 6-12, with remission achievable in 50-70% of patients by month 6-12. Psoriatic arthritis and AS: NSAID response within 2-4 weeks, biologic response within 12-16 weeks. Osteoarthritis: structured exercise programs show measurable pain and function gains within 8-12 weeks and grow with continued adherence. Total joint replacement: most pain relief by 6 weeks, full functional recovery by 6-12 months.
complications: Progressive joint destruction and deformity — preventable across inflammatory types with treat-to-target DMARD therapy
Cardiovascular disease — risk roughly 1.5-2x age-matched controls in RA, AS, gout and psoriatic arthritis; partly mediated by chronic inflammation
Osteoporosis and fragility fracture — driven by chronic inflammation, reduced activity and corticosteroid use; bone-density monitoring is standard in inflammatory arthritis
Anterior uveitis in 25-40% of ankylosing spondylitis and some JIA patients — needs same-day ophthalmology assessment when symptomatic
Increased infection risk on biologic and JAK inhibitor therapy — addressed by pre-treatment TB and hepatitis screening, vaccination, and prompt evaluation of fevers
Mental health comorbidity — depression and anxiety prevalence is roughly twice the general population and worsens pain and function; screening and treatment are part of standard care

supportResources.name: CDC Arthritis Program
supportResources.description: US surveillance data, patient education and the Walk With Ease and Arthritis Foundation self-management programs.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that writes the major arthritis guidelines, including disease-specific fact sheets.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient community, exercise programs, lifestyle resources and advocacy across all arthritis types.

supportResources.name: EULAR — European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology
supportResources.description: European patient education, current treatment recommendations and shared decision-making resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue and cognitive symptoms without joint swelling, redness, warmth or laboratory inflammation. Inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) and joint imaging are normal. The two can coexist, and patients with active arthritis often develop a secondary fibromyalgia syndrome that responds to different treatments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PMR produces bilateral shoulder and hip-girdle pain and stiffness in adults over 50, with markedly elevated ESR or CRP, and dramatic response to low-dose prednisolone (15-20 mg daily) within 48-72 hours. Joint imaging shows bursitis and tenosynovitis rather than synovitis or erosions. It can mimic seronegative RA — anti-CCP testing helps distinguish.

confusedWithConditions.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Carpal tunnel syndrome causes numbness, tingling and weakness in the thumb, index and middle fingers from median nerve compression at the wrist — not joint inflammation. Symptoms worsen at night and with wrist flexion; nerve conduction studies confirm the diagnosis. It can coexist with rheumatoid arthritis when synovitis compresses the nerve.

confusedWithConditions.name: Reactive Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Reactive arthritis develops 1-4 weeks after a gastrointestinal or urogenital infection, typically affects the lower limbs asymmetrically, and often includes eye (conjunctivitis or uveitis) and urinary symptoms. Most cases resolve within 3-12 months. Establishing the antecedent infection and HLA-B27 status separates it from the chronic spondyloarthritides.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common form of arthritis — wear-and-tear degeneration of cartilage. Distinct mechanism but the same final-pathway symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: The prototype autoimmune inflammatory arthritis. Symmetric small-joint disease that responds to early DMARD therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common crystal-deposition arthritis in adults. Fully controllable with urate-lowering therapy held below 6.0 mg/dL.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Affects roughly 30% of people with psoriasis. Shares biologic treatment options with rheumatoid arthritis and ankylosing spondylitis.

relatedConditions.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The prototype axial spondyloarthropathy — inflammation of the sacroiliac joints and spine, strongly linked to HLA-B27.

faqs.question: What is arthritis?
faqs.answer: Arthritis is the umbrella term for over 100 conditions that cause joint pain, stiffness, swelling or damage. The most common forms are osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, gout and the spondyloarthropathies (psoriatic, ankylosing, reactive). Each type has its own cause, tests and treatment.

faqs.question: How do I know which type of arthritis I have?
faqs.answer: The type is identified from joint pattern, age at onset, morning stiffness duration, systemic features, blood tests (RF, anti-CCP, ESR, CRP, uric acid, HLA-B27) and imaging. A rheumatologist usually narrows the diagnosis at the first visit.

faqs.question: What are the early signs of arthritis?
faqs.answer: Morning stiffness lasting over 30 minutes, persistent joint swelling, pain that disturbs sleep, reduced grip strength, and unexplained fatigue with joint symptoms. Inflammatory back pain that improves with movement in someone under 45 is an early sign of axial spondyloarthritis.

faqs.question: Is arthritis curable?
faqs.answer: Most chronic arthritis is controllable but not curable. Rheumatoid, psoriatic and ankylosing arthritis can reach remission on DMARDs and biologics. Gout is fully controllable at urate below 6.0 mg/dL. Osteoarthritis is managed with exercise, weight loss and joint replacement when needed.

faqs.question: What is the difference between osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis?
faqs.answer: Osteoarthritis is mechanical cartilage breakdown that usually appears after age 50 and worsens with activity. Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune attack on the synovium that causes symmetric small-joint swelling, over an hour of morning stiffness, and positive anti-CCP or rheumatoid factor.

faqs.question: Can arthritis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Some types largely yes. Healthy weight, regular exercise, avoiding joint injury and treating cardiometabolic risk factors lower osteoarthritis and gout risk. Stopping smoking halves rheumatoid arthritis risk in anti-CCP-positive people. Autoimmune types cannot be primary-prevented.

faqs.question: What blood tests diagnose arthritis?
faqs.answer: Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP for RA, ESR and CRP for systemic inflammation, serum uric acid for gout, ANA for lupus, and HLA-B27 when spondyloarthropathy is suspected. No single test diagnoses arthritis; results are read alongside history, examination and imaging.

faqs.question: Does arthritis only affect older adults?
faqs.answer: No. Osteoarthritis is more common after 50, but rheumatoid arthritis often starts between 30 and 60, ankylosing spondylitis before 45, gout in men from the 30s, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis before age 16. About 8 million US adults aged 18-44 have arthritis (CDC).

faqs.question: Can children get arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis affects about 1 in 1,000 children. Subtypes include oligoarticular, polyarticular, systemic (Still disease), enthesitis-related and juvenile psoriatic. Many reach drug-free remission with prompt DMARD therapy; uveitis screening is mandatory in several subtypes.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with arthritis?
faqs.answer: Limit ultra-processed foods, sugar-sweetened beverages, high-fructose corn syrup, excess red and processed meat, and high amounts of beer or spirits — especially with gout. A Mediterranean-style diet of olive oil, fish, vegetables and whole grains is supported across arthritis types.

faqs.question: Does cracking my knuckles cause arthritis?
faqs.answer: No. Long-term studies (Castellanos 1990; Boutin 2017 imaging) show no link between knuckle cracking and osteoarthritis. The sound is gas-bubble cavitation in synovial fluid, not joint damage. Knuckle cracking may slightly reduce grip strength but does not cause arthritis.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe with arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular exercise is one of the most effective treatments for almost every type of arthritis. Aerobic, strength and flexibility training reduce pain and improve function. During an acute flare switch to gentle range-of-motion or aquatic exercise and resume full programming once swelling settles.

faqs.question: Can weight loss improve arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Losing 5-10% of body weight measurably reduces knee osteoarthritis pain, improves response to rheumatoid arthritis medications, and lowers serum urate in gout. Each kilogram lost reduces knee load by roughly four kilograms during walking. Diet plus exercise outperforms either alone.

faqs.question: What is the most common type of arthritis?
faqs.answer: Osteoarthritis is most common, affecting about 32.5 million US adults and over 595 million people worldwide (GBD 2019). It is followed by gout (about 4% of US adults), rheumatoid arthritis (around 0.5%), and psoriatic and ankylosing spondylitis (each 0.3-0.5%).

faqs.question: When should I see a rheumatologist?
faqs.answer: See a rheumatologist for joint swelling lasting over six weeks, morning stiffness over an hour, symmetric small-joint involvement, inflammatory back pain under age 45, or family history of autoimmune arthritis. Urgent referral is needed for a hot joint with fever (rule out septic arthritis).

faqs.question: Will I need to take medication for life?
faqs.answer: It depends on the type. Gout, rheumatoid, psoriatic and ankylosing arthritis usually need long-term medication; stopping often leads to flares within 12 months. Reactive and septic arthritis are treated for weeks to months. Osteoarthritis uses intermittent symptomatic medication instead.

faqs.question: How much does arthritis treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Generic NSAIDs, allopurinol, colchicine, methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine often cost under USD 20 per month. Biologic DMARDs list at USD 20,000-65,000 per year in the US, with biosimilars and patient-assistance programs lowering out-of-pocket costs. Joint replacement averages USD 30,000-50,000.

faqs.question: Can stress trigger arthritis flares?
faqs.answer: Psychological stress is linked to flares in rheumatoid, psoriatic and ankylosing arthritis through cortisol disruption, sleep loss and cytokine changes. Stress alone does not cause arthritis but worsens activity. Mindfulness and CBT show small but measurable benefits in trials.

faqs.question: What is the difference between arthritis and arthralgia?
faqs.answer: Arthritis means joint inflammation or damage — usually with swelling, warmth or imaging changes. Arthralgia means joint pain without those signs. Many viral illnesses cause arthralgia without arthritis. Persistent arthralgia with stiffness or raised inflammatory markers deserves rheumatology review.

faqs.question: Can arthritis affect organs other than joints?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially in inflammatory types. Rheumatoid arthritis can affect lungs, eyes, skin, heart and blood vessels. Psoriatic and ankylosing arthritis are linked to uveitis, skin disease and inflammatory bowel disease. Gout affects kidneys and raises cardiovascular risk.

faqs.question: Are there any new treatments for arthritis?
faqs.answer: Recent additions include IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab) for psoriatic arthritis, JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib) across multiple types, and pegloticase for refractory gout. Research is active in cartilage repair, CAR-T therapy and precision matching.

metaDescription: Arthritis: 100+ joint conditions including osteoarthritis, RA, gout and spondyloarthritis. Symptoms, types and treatment reviewed by our medical board.
